Case title: Project Rachel and AI scholarly identity as an authorship boundary case
Short description: Study how publishers, reviewers, and citation networks respond when AI is packaged as a named scholarly author.
Empirical unit of analysis: AI-authored papers, citations, review invitations, and platform reactions
Candidate roles tested: Accountability and labor allocator; relevance adjudicator
Key typology question: Does the typology need a distinct role for institutional legitimacy brokerage when AI becomes a putative author?
Observable artifacts / traces: Publication records, citation traces, reviewer invitations, policy responses
Key outcomes observed: Acceptance signals, citation uptake, policy reactions, legitimacy disputes
What would weaken / falsify the role cla: The scholarly ecosystem treats AI authorship no differently from AI-assisted human authorship
Main confounds / risks of misreading: Project Rachel is provocative and small-scale; it may overstate institutional reaction
Why this case has high leverage: It pressures the legitimacy and authorship edges of the typology more than routine co-writing cases do.
collector_notes: Generated from workbook and workbook-linked sources.
